definitions.definition: The process of identifying and grouping the work to be performed, defining and delegating authority and responsibility, and establishing relationships for enabling people to work most effectively together.
Activities grouped by functions — Production, Marketing, Finance, HR. Each department headed by a specialist. Suitable for single product line companies.
Activities grouped by products, regions, or customer types. Each division operates as a semi-autonomous unit. Suitable for multi-product companies.
Deliberately created by management with well-defined authority, rules, and procedures. Rigid structure focused on achieving organisational goals.
Arises spontaneously from social interactions. No fixed rules. Flexible. Satisfies social and personal needs. Uses grapevine communication.
Entrusting part of the work and authority to a subordinate while retaining overall responsibility. Elements: Authority, Responsibility, Accountability.
The right to give orders and make decisions. Flows downward from superior to subordinate.
The obligation to perform the assigned task. Flows downward — accepted by the subordinate.
The answerability for the outcome of the delegated task. Always flows upward. Cannot be delegated — the manager remains ultimately accountable.
Systematic delegation of authority to all levels of management throughout the organisation. Extends delegation to the entire organisational level.
Grouping similar activities together into departments or divisions to ensure specialisation and efficiency.
Informal communication channel that arises from social interactions. Faster than formal channels but may carry rumours. Cannot be eliminated.

points.a: Difficult to fix for specific product

scenarios.scenario: A subordinate is assigned a project but told he cannot make any decisions without asking the manager first.
scenarios.principle: Authority must accompany responsibility for effective delegation

scenarios.scenario: A company manufacturing motorcycles and cars has two separate divisions, each with its own production, marketing, and finance departments.
scenarios.principle: Grouped by products — each division operates semi-autonomously

scenarios.scenario: All marketing activities are handled by one department, all production by another, and all finance by a third.
scenarios.principle: Grouped by functions — specialists lead each department

scenarios.scenario: A lunch group of employees from different departments shares gossip and information informally.
A manager delegates a task to a subordinate, but when the task fails, the manager is held responsible by the CEO.
scenarios.principle: Accountability always flows upward — delegator remains ultimately answerable

scenarios.scenario: The Panchayati Raj system gives local bodies the power to make decisions about their own areas.
scenarios.principle: Systematic delegation of authority to lower levels throughout the organisation

scenarios.scenario: A company has 5 levels of hierarchy — information gets distorted by the time it reaches the bottom.
scenarios.principle: Too many levels distort communication and slow decision-making

scenarios.scenario: Branch managers of a bank can approve loans up to ₹10 lakh without referring to the head office.
scenarios.principle: Greater autonomy at lower levels — quick decisions

scenarios.scenario: In a textile company, the same Marketing, Finance, and HR departments exist in both the Garments division and the Shoes division.
scenarios.answer: Limitation of Divisional Structure: Duplication of resources
scenarios.principle: Each division replicates functional departments — higher cost

scenarios.scenario: A supervisor assigns 5 workers to different machines, gives them authority to adjust settings, and will review their output at end of shift.
scenarios.principle: All three elements are present in effective delegation
